title: SEGMENT 1 — What fluorescent proteins are, and why they changed biology

lines.text: SEGMENT 1 — What fluorescent proteins are, and why they changed biology
Alright, let's get started.
Good morning, everyone.
For the next two hours we're going to talk about fluorescent proteins.
And I want to begin by telling you, plainly, that the molecules we're about to discuss are probably the single most important tool to enter cell biology in the last fifty years.
That's a big claim.
By the end of this lecture I want you to believe it, and to understand why.
Here's the analogy I want you to carry through the whole lecture.
A cell is, in a sense, a tiny theater — a stage on which a play is constantly being performed.
Proteins move, vesicles traffic, organelles divide, signals fire, chromosomes condense and split apart.
That's the play.
And for most of the history of biology, we could not watch the play happen.
We could grind the cell up and ask which actors were in the cast.
We could fix a cell — freeze it in formaldehyde — and take a single still photograph of one frozen instant.
But we couldn't sit in the audience and watch the action unfold in a living, breathing cell.
Fluorescent proteins changed that.
They gave biology a stage light.
More specifically, they gave us molecular glow sticks that the cell makes for itself.
You hand the cell a gene — a piece of DNA encoding a glowing protein — and the cell builds the glow stick from scratch, with its own ribosomes, out of ordinary amino acids.

Then you attach that glow stick to whatever protein you want to watch — your favorite receptor, your favorite enzyme, your favorite cytoskeletal element — and now you can sit at the microscope and follow that protein through the cell, in real time, in a living cell, for hours.
Let me sharpen what that shift meant.
Before fluorescent proteins, you had two options if you wanted to know where a protein lived inside a cell.
Option one was to kill the cell — fix it, permeabilize it, and stain it with a dye-labeled antibody.
That works, and it still gets used every day in labs around the world.
But the price is enormous: your cell is dead.
You see one snapshot of one frozen instant, and you have no idea what happened a second before or a second after.
Imagine trying to understand a soccer game by looking at one still photograph.
You can identify the players.
You cannot tell who is winning, or what just happened, or what's about to happen.
Option two was to purify your protein, stick a dye on it in a test tube, and then literally inject the labeled protein back into a living cell with a tiny glass needle.
That's brutally difficult, the cell has to be big enough to inject, and you have no control over where the protein goes once it's inside.
Almost nobody did it routinely.
Fluorescent proteins broke that wall open.
With a fluorescent protein, the labeling happens from the inside.
You give the cell the recipe, the cell builds the glowing tag, and every single copy of your protein comes out of the ribosome with the glow stick already attached.
The labeling is perfect — every molecule is tagged, no more, no fewer.
The cell is alive.
You can watch the play happen.
That shift, from snapshot to movie, from dead to alive, from approximate to exact, is the entire reason this field exists.
It is genuinely the most consequential reagent change in cell biology in the last half-century.
If you walk out of here in two hours believing that, and able to explain to a friend at the pub why it matters, we've succeeded.
Now, why does this matter beyond just being able to see things?
Because biology is a process.
A cell isn't a static object — it's a system that is constantly changing.
The interesting questions in cell biology are almost always about change over time.
How does this signal travel from the membrane to the nucleus?
How does this vesicle find its destination?
How does this complex assemble and then fall apart?
Those are questions about movement, about timing, about dynamics.
And to answer questions about dynamics, you have to watch the dynamics happen.
Snapshots will not do.
So hold this picture in your head for the rest of the lecture: a fluorescent protein is a stage light.
It tells the microscope where to look.
And because the cell builds it for itself from a gene we provide, the labeling is exquisitely specific, alive, and reusable across every cell type and every organism we can put DNA into.
That is the central magic.
Everything else we'll cover today — the colors, the structure, the sensors, the microscopes — is an elaboration on that one core capability.
One more thing before we move on.
This is also one of the great human stories in modern biology, and we're going to tell it.
It begins with a chemist who spent decades collecting nearly a million jellyfish by hand.
It runs through a worm biologist who realized one gene could make any cell glow.
It passes through a chemistry lab in San Diego that built a rainbow palette out of a single jellyfish protein.
And it ends — well, it hasn't ended.
New variants come out every year.
Let's begin at the beginning.
